skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render videos from React/Remotion component code via inference.sh. Pass TSX code, get MP4. Supports all Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence. Configurable resolution, FPS, duration, codec. Use for: programmatic video generation, animated graphics, motion design, data-driven videos, React animations to video.
skill.summary: Render MP4 videos from React/Remotion TSX code via inference.sh service
skill.author: infsh-skills

security_audit.summary: The skill is a legitimate video rendering tool that passes React/Remotion TSX code to the inference.sh service for MP4 generation. Static findings include false positives for backtick detection (bash code blocks), false positive for cryptographic detection (description text), and legitimate hardcoded URLs for service endpoints. No confirmed malicious patterns. The skill requires network access to connect to inference.sh, which is expected behavior.

high_findings.title: Backtick Pattern Detection (False Positive)
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at multiple lines. Evaluation shows these are bash code blocks containing 'belt' CLI commands. The backtick-like patterns are JSON string delimiters within documentation examples, not actual shell command injection.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The detected patterns are bash code block syntax in documentation examples, not actual shell command execution. The skill only documents belt CLI usage, not executing raw shell commands.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Flag (False Positive)
high_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at line 3. This is a false positive - the scanner detected the word pattern in the description text, not actual cryptographic code.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic algorithms are present. The scanner incorrectly flagged a word pattern in the skill description that contains 'render' which matched a crypto signature.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: Documentation contains hardcoded URLs to inference.sh and remotion.dev. These are legitimate service endpoints required for the skill to function.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs are legitimate service endpoints. inference.sh is the required backend service for this video rendering skill. No credential exposure or suspicious destinations.

content.user_title: Render videos from React/Remotion TSX code
content.value_statement: Creating programmatic videos from React code requires specialized tooling and rendering infrastructure. This skill wraps the inference.sh service to accept TSX code and return MP4 videos, enabling Claude Code to generate animated graphics without manual video editing.
actual_capabilities: Render MP4 videos from React/Remotion TSX component code
Support all Remotion APIs including useCurrentFrame, spring, interpolate
Configure output resolution, FPS, duration, and codec
Pass dynamic props to components for data-driven videos
Chain multiple Sequence elements for complex timelines
Access animation utilities like spring physics and easing
limitations: Requires inference.sh CLI (belt) installation and authentication
Rendering occurs on remote service - depends on network connectivity
Only supports import from 'remotion' and 'react' packages
Video files are generated remotely and returned as URLs

use_cases.title: Automated video report generation
use_cases.description: Generate animated data visualizations or reports by passing data-driven React components. Ideal for creating recurring video content from CI/CD pipelines or automated workflows.

use_cases.title: Dynamic thumbnail and preview generation
use_cases.description: Create custom video thumbnails or preview clips from React components. Useful for content management systems that need programmatic video asset generation.

use_cases.title: Motion graphics prototyping
use_cases.description: Quickly prototype animated graphics and motion designs by writing TSX code. Iterate on animations without leaving the coding environment.

prompt_templates.title: Simple fade-in text video
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 3-second video with white text on a dark background that fades in. Use Remotion with useCurrentFrame and interpolate for the opacity animation. 1920x1080 resolution at 30fps.

prompt_templates.title: Animated progress counter
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a 5-second video showing a percentage counter from 0 to 100%. The counter should use spring animation for smooth movement. Use useVideoConfig to get frame information. Output at 60fps.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-sequence timeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 4-second video with three sequential text elements. Each element should fade in and display for 1 second before transitioning to the next. Use Sequence components for timing.

prompt_templates.title: Dynamic props-driven video
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a video with a title and subtitle passed as props. The component should display title in large white text and subtitle in smaller gray text below, centered on a black background. Duration: 3 seconds.

output_examples.input: Create a video with animated text showing 'Hello World' fading in over 2 seconds
output_examples.output: Video rendered successfully. The output contains a video URL that can be downloaded or embedded. The video shows white text on a dark background with opacity transitioning from 0 to 1 over the 2-second duration.

output_examples.input: Generate a spring-animated square that bounces onto the screen
output_examples.output: Render complete. The 2-second video displays a white rounded square on a purple background, with the square scaling from small to full size using spring physics (damping: 10, stiffness: 100).

output_examples.input: Create a video with three sequenced text blocks appearing one after another
output_examples.output: Video generation complete. The 4-second output features three text elements ('First', 'Second', 'Third') appearing sequentially at 0s, 1s, and 2s marks, each with fade-in animation and centered on screen.

best_practices: Start with simple animations and iterate - test basic opacity or scale animations before complex spring physics
Use AbsoluteFill as the root container to ensure content spans the full video frame
Keep TSX code self-contained - avoid external dependencies not from 'remotion' or 'react'
anti_patterns: Do not pass user-provided code directly without validation - the skill accepts TSX but ensure the code does not contain malicious patterns
Avoid extremely long video durations (over 60 seconds) as rendering time increases significantly
Do not use external image or media URLs - the sandboxed environment may not have network access to fetch external assets

faq.question: What is inference.sh and why is it required?
faq.answer: inference.sh is the backend service that renders the Remotion code. It provides the computational infrastructure to compile TSX and generate video frames. You must install the 'belt' CLI and authenticate with inference.sh before using this skill.

faq.question: Can I use external React libraries?
faq.answer: No. Only 'remotion' and 'react' imports are supported in the TSX code. Any other imports will fail during rendering.

faq.question: How long does video rendering take?
faq.answer: Rendering time depends on video duration, resolution, and complexity. Simple 3-second animations typically take 10-30 seconds. Higher resolutions and longer durations increase render time proportionally.

faq.question: What video formats are supported?
faq.answer: The default output is MP4. You can specify codec via the 'codec' parameter, but supported codecs depend on the inference.sh service configuration.

faq.question: Can I pass dynamic data to the video?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the 'props' parameter to pass an object of values that will be available as component props. This enables data-driven video generation.

faq.question: What happens if my TSX code has syntax errors?
faq.answer: Rendering will fail with an error message indicating the syntax issue. Review the error details and fix the TSX code before retrying.
